Targets_Medium_Confidence: GPR55, GPR18, TRPV1, TRPV2, PPARG, PPARA
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: CB1 receptor (CNS, primary), CB2 receptor (peripheral/immune), GPR55, TRPV1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Partial agonist at CB1; psychoactivity from CB1 in cortico-limbic regions; CB2 mediates immunomodulatory effects
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved as dronabinol (Marinol, Syndros) for CINV and AIDS anorexia. Nabiximols (Sativex, ex-US) for MS spasticity. Trials in chronic pain, sleep, PTSD.

Mode_of_Action: Receptor partial agonist (CB1, CB2) — lower potency than Δ9
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: CB1 (lower affinity vs Δ9-THC), CB2
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Isomer of Δ9-THC; reportedly ~50–75% as psychoactive; greater chemical stability
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Unregulated in many US states post-2018 Farm Bill (hemp-derived). No FDA-approved indications. Pediatric antiemetic study (Abrahamov, 1995).

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: COX-1/COX-2 (weak inhibitor), TRPA1 agonist, PPAR-γ; weak CB1/CB2
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Non-psychoactive precursor that decarboxylates to Δ9-THC on heating; carboxylate prevents BBB penetration as agonist
Clinical_Trials_Indications: No approved indications. Preclinical interest: neuroprotection, anti-inflammatory, anti-emetic. No active trials.

Mode_of_Action: Negative allosteric modulator (CB1); 5-HT1A agonist; TRPV1 agonist
Targets_Medium_Confidence: CNR1, CNR2, ADORA2A, PPARG, FAAH, GPR18
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: CB1 (NAM), CB2, 5-HT1A, TRPV1, GPR55, PPAR-γ, adenosine uptake inhibition
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Indirect endocannabinoid system modulator; anticonvulsant via multiple targets; anxiolytic via 5-HT1A
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved as Epidiolex for Dravet syndrome, Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, tuberous sclerosis complex seizures. Trials in anxiety, schizophrenia, opioid use disorder, autism irritability.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: Muscarinic acetylcholine receptors M1–M5 (non-selective antagonist); minor antihistaminic activity
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Competitive muscarinic antagonist crossing BBB; produces anticholinergic delirium and anti-motion-sickness effects
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved transdermal patch (Transderm Scōp) for motion sickness and postoperative nausea. IV single-dose investigated as rapid antidepressant in MDD (Phase 2).

Mode_of_Action: Prodrug; metabolized to GHB via ADH/ALDH
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: Pharmacology = GHB after hepatic conversion (ADH → GHB-aldehyde → GHB by ALDH)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Industrial chemical (solvent, plasticizer); metabolized to GHB in 5–15 min via alcohol dehydrogenase pathway
Clinical_Trials_Indications: List I chemical (US); not federally scheduled. Misused as GHB substitute. No therapeutic use.

Mode_of_Action: Prodrug; rapidly hydrolyzed to GHB by serum lactonase
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: Pharmacology = GHB after hydrolysis (paraoxonase serum esterase opens lactone)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Faster onset than GHB itself due to better lipid solubility and rapid hydrolysis to GHB
Clinical_Trials_Indications: List I chemical (US). Industrial solvent. Misused as GHB substitute; no therapeutic use.

Mode_of_Action: Receptor agonist (GHB receptor; weak GABA-B agonist)
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: GHB receptor (high affinity), GABA-B receptor (low affinity); modulates dopamine release
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Endogenous metabolite of GABA; biphasic dose-response: stimulant at low doses (via DA), sedative at high (via GABA-B)
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule III) as sodium oxybate (Xyrem, Lumryz) for narcolepsy with cataplexy. Schedule I when illicit. Trials in fibromyalgia, alcohol withdrawal.

Mode_of_Action: Positive allosteric modulator (GABA-A receptor, β subunit site)
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: GABA-A receptor (PAM at β-subunit site distinct from benzodiazepine site), H1 antihistamine activity
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Mandrax (with diphenhydramine) and Quaalude were widely abused 1960s–70s; mechanism distinct from benzodiazepines
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 1984); withdrawn worldwide. No current clinical trials.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA (PCP analog), σ1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: N-ethyl analog of PCP; similar potency, slightly shorter duration
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). 1970s designer drug; no medical use.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA receptor (PCP analog with pyrrolidine vs piperidine)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: PCP analog with reduced ring size; comparable dissociative effects
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). No medical use.

Mode_of_Action: Receptor antagonist (NMDA) — more potent than PCP
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA receptor (thiophene PCP analog, higher affinity), σ1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Thiophene replaces phenyl ring; longer duration and higher potency than PCP
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). No medical use.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA receptor (PCP analog)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Pyrrolidine analog of TCP
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). No medical use.

Targets_Medium_Confidence: HCN1, OPRK1, OPRM1, OPRD1, HTR2A, DRD2, CHRM1, CHRM2
Targets_Low_Confidence: SLC6A3, SLC6A2, SLC6A4, SIGMAR1, TRPV1, GABRA1
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA receptor (non-competitive antagonist, primary); HCN1; κ/μ opioid receptors; monoaminergic and cholinergic at high dose
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Open-channel NMDA antagonist binding PCP site; rapid antidepressant effect via AMPA-mediated synaptogenesis and BDNF release
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved as anesthetic (1970) and esketamine nasal spray (Spravato, 2019) for treatment-resistant depression. Active trials: PTSD, OCD, chronic pain, suicidality, alcohol use disorder.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA (non-competitive antagonist, primary), σ1, DAT (weak), nicotinic acetylcholine receptors
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Open-channel NMDA blocker (PCP/dizocilpine binding site within channel pore); precipitates schizophreniform symptoms
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule II (US); withdrawn 1965 as anesthetic Sernyl. No current therapeutic use.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA receptor (weak)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Primary amine PCP precursor; can be alkylated to PCP/PHP
Clinical_Trials_Indications: List I chemical (US). No therapeutic use.

Mode_of_Action: PCP synthesis intermediate; minimal direct activity
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: Hydrolyzes to PCP
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: α-aminonitrile intermediate in classical PCP synthesis (Bruylants reaction)
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule II List I chemical (US). No therapeutic use.

Mode_of_Action: Receptor antagonist (NMDA), kappa-opioid agonist, SERT inhibitor
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: NMDA antagonist; σ2 agonist; κ/μ opioid receptors; SERT/NET inhibition; nicotinic α3β4 antagonist
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Iboga alkaloid; metabolite noribogaine is long-acting at SERT and κ-opioid receptors and may underlie anti-addictive effects
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). Phase 1/2 trials in Canada, Brazil, Spain for opioid use disorder and treatment-resistant depression. Used in unregulated clinics worldwide.

Mode_of_Action: Releaser (SERT, NET, DAT); 5-HT2A agonist; weak MAOI
Targets_Medium_Confidence: HTR2A, HTR1A, HTR2B, HTR2C, OXT, AVP
Targets_Low_Confidence: ADRA1A, ADRA2A, DRD2, CHRM1, HRH1, MAOA
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: SERT (primary releaser), NET, DAT; downstream oxytocin release; 5-HT2A direct agonism (weak)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Reverses monoamine transporters via TAAR1 signaling and intracellular release from VMAT2 vesicles
Clinical_Trials_Indications: FDA rejected Lykos PTSD NDA (Aug 2024) but Breakthrough Therapy designation retained. Phase 3 PTSD trials reopened; investigation in alcohol use disorder and autism-related social anxiety.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: SERT, NET, DAT (MDMA-like profile, slightly more SERT-selective)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: N-ethyl homolog of MDMA; shorter, less euphoric subjective effects
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 1988). No clinical trials. Recreational use as 'Eve' in 1980s–90s.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: SERT, NET, DAT, 5-HT2A, TAAR1 (more psychedelic than MDMA)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Major active metabolite of MDMA; produced via N-demethylation by CYP2D6
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 1985). Studied in psychiatry in 1960s as 'love drug'. No active clinical trials.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT, NET, VMAT2, TAAR1, weak MAO inhibition
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Combination of 4 amphetamine salts gives smoother PK profile than pure d-amphetamine; L-isomer adds NE-selective component
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for ADHD (Adderall, Adderall XR, Mydayis) and narcolepsy. First-line ADHD pharmacotherapy in adults.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT, NET, VMAT2; β-ketone analog of methamphetamine
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: β-keto group reduces lipophilicity vs methamphetamine but increases stability; potent DA/NE releaser
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 1993). Used briefly in 1930s–40s Soviet Union as antidepressant ('Ephedrone'). No approved use.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT (D-isomer more selective for DA than L-), NET, VMAT2, TAAR1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: D-amphetamine is 3–4x more potent at DAT than L-amphetamine; primarily dopaminergic profile
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for ADHD and narcolepsy (Dexedrine, Zenzedi, ProCentra).

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT, NET, VMAT2 (long-acting amphetamine-like)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Cyclized aminorex analog; ~16 h half-life; risk of pulmonary hypertension (aminorex family)
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 1987). Briefly investigated as anorectic; never approved. No active trials.

Mode_of_Action: Reuptake inhibitor (DAT, NET), releaser (DA, NE) via VMAT2 and TAAR1
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT, NET, VMAT2, TAAR1, weak MAO inhibition
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Substrate-type releaser; reverses transporters via PKC- and TAAR1-mediated phosphorylation; depletes vesicular monoamines
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for ADHD and narcolepsy (Adderall, Evekeo, Adzenys, Mydayis, Dyanavel). Mixed salts (75/25 D/L) marketed as Adderall.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT (primary), NET, VMAT2, TAAR1, σ1, MAO inhibition
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: N-methylation increases lipophilicity and BBB penetration vs amphetamine; longer duration and greater abuse potential
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) as Desoxyn for ADHD (rarely prescribed) and short-term obesity. Schedule II; primary recreational/illicit stimulant globally.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT, NET, VMAT2; pharmacology similar to amphetamine, weaker
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: N-ethyl analog of amphetamine; reduced potency at DAT/NET vs the methyl analog
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Discontinued anorectic (Apetinil, 1950s–60s). Schedule I in US; controlled in most jurisdictions. No current approved use.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT, NET, SERT (mixed releaser/inhibitor); ~10x weaker than amphetamine
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Originally investigated as antihelmintic; recreational stimulant popular in NZ before scheduling
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 2002). Briefly legal MDMA alternative ('Party Pills'). No approved therapeutic use.

Mode_of_Action: Reuptake inhibitor (DAT, NET) — blocks reuptake without releasing
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT (primary), NET; mechanism distinct from amphetamine (no VMAT2 reversal)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Pure reuptake inhibitor (vs amphetamine which also releases); D-threo enantiomer (dexmethylphenidate) is active form
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for ADHD and narcolepsy (Ritalin, Ritalin LA, Concerta, Methylin, Daytrana patch, Quillivant XR). First-line ADHD treatment in children.

Mode_of_Action: Reuptake inhibitor (DAT, NET, SERT); voltage-gated sodium channel blocker
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: DAT (primary reinforcing), NET, SERT, voltage-gated Na+ channels, σ-receptors
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Tropane alkaloid; binds monoamine transporters in an inward-facing conformation distinct from amphetamines
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule II (US) for topical ENT anesthesia (4% solution). No oral therapeutic use. Vaccine and pharmacotherapy trials for cocaine use disorder (modafinil, disulfiram, topiramate) — none approved.

Mode_of_Action: Receptor agonist (μ, partial); monoamine reuptake inhibitor
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (partial), weak NET/DAT/SERT inhibition
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Mixed opioid agonist + monoamine reuptake inhibitor; sometimes classed as 'atypical opioid'
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule IV in some countries. Marketed as Santenol in Japan/Italy as analgesic; not in US.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (via active metabolites nor-LAAM, dinor-LAAM)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Long half-life (2–6 days for active metabolites) allowed 3-day-a-week dosing for OUD
Clinical_Trials_Indications: FDA-approved 1993 for opioid use disorder (ORLAAM); voluntarily withdrawn 2003 due to QT prolongation, torsades de pointes.

Mode_of_Action: Prodrug; metabolized to 6-MAM and morphine, μ-opioid agonist
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (via 6-MAM and morphine), δ-opioid receptor, κ-opioid receptor
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Lipophilic prodrug; crosses BBB rapidly; hydrolyzed by carboxylesterases to active 6-monoacetylmorphine and morphine
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). Approved as diamorphine in UK, Canada, Switzerland for severe acute pain and palliative care. Heroin-assisted treatment programs (Switzerland, Netherlands, Germany) for refractory OUD.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (full agonist; 5–7x morphine), δ (weak)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Ketone analog of morphine; greater lipid solubility than morphine drives faster onset
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for moderate-to-severe pain (Dilaudid, Exalgo ER). Used in palliative care and post-operative pain.

Mode_of_Action: Receptor agonist (μ, full; weak κ activity)
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (primary), κ (weak; clinically debated), δ (very weak)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Bioactivated by CYP2D6 to oxymorphone (10–20x μ-affinity); CYP3A4 inactivates to noroxycodone
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for moderate-to-severe pain (OxyContin, Percocet, Roxicodone). Central driver of US opioid crisis; abuse-deterrent OxyContin reformulation 2010.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (primary, full agonist; ~80–100x morphine), δ and κ (weak)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: High-affinity μ-agonist with rapid BBB penetration; fast onset and short duration when administered IV/transmucosal
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for severe acute and chronic pain (Duragesic patch, Actiq lozenge, Subsys spray, Sublimaze IV). Anesthesia adjunct. Illicit analogs (carfentanil, acetylfentanyl) drive overdose epidemic.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (meperidine analog, ~70x potency)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Synthesis byproduct MPTP (1980s) caused permanent Parkinsonism in users (Langston, 1983 — 'frozen addicts' case)
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). No medical use.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor (meperidine analog)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: MPPP analog with phenethyl substituent; similar MPTP contamination risk
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). No medical use.

Mode_of_Action: Receptor agonist (μ); SERT inhibitor; weak NMDA antagonist
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: μ-opioid receptor, SERT, NMDA receptor (weak), muscarinic (weak antagonist)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: First fully synthetic opioid (1939); active metabolite normeperidine is neurotoxic and pro-convulsant
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Approved (Schedule II) for moderate-to-severe pain (Demerol). Use declining due to seizure risk, serotonin syndrome with MAOIs/SSRIs, short duration.

Targets_Medium_Confidence: HTR1B, HTR1D, HTR2B, HTR5A, HTR7, DRD1, DRD2, DRD3
Targets_Low_Confidence: DRD4, DRD5, ADRA1A, ADRA2A, TAAR1, ADRB1
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: 5-HT2A receptor (primary), 5-HT1A, 5-HT2C, dopamine D2, TAAR1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Partial agonist at 5-HT2A with extremely slow off-rate (residence-time hypothesis); β-arrestin2 signaling implicated
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 1970). Phase 2 trials: anxiety in life-threatening illness (MAPS, MindMed), generalized anxiety disorder. Re-emerging psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy research.

Mode_of_Action: Prodrug; psilocin is partial agonist at 5-HT2A
Targets_Medium_Confidence: HTR1B, HTR1D, HTR1E, HTR2B, HTR5A, HTR6, HTR7
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: 5-HT2A receptor (primary), 5-HT2C, 5-HT1A (active form is psilocin)
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Dephosphorylated in vivo to psilocin (4-OH-DMT); 5-HT2A partial agonism on cortical pyramidal neurons drives psychedelic effects
Clinical_Trials_Indications: FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for treatment-resistant depression (COMPASS Pathways). Active Phase 2/3: MDD, alcohol use disorder, anorexia, end-of-life anxiety, smoking cessation.

Targets_Medium_Confidence: HTR1B, HTR1D, HTR1E, HTR2B, HTR5A, HTR6, HTR7, TAAR1
Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: 5-HT2A (primary), 5-HT1A, 5-HT2C, σ1 receptor, TAAR1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Endogenous trace amine; rapid onset/offset via inhaled or i.v. route; ayahuasca combines DMT with reversible MAO-A inhibitor β-carbolines
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). Phase 1/2 trials for depression (Small Pharma SPL026, Algernon AP-188). Ayahuasca studied for treatment-resistant depression.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: 5-HT2A (partial agonist), 5-HT2C, TAAR1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Shulgin phenethylamine; lower 5-HT2A efficacy than DOI/DOB; produces shorter psychedelic experience than LSD
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). No formal clinical trials. Use in some unregulated psychedelic-assisted therapy practices.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: 5-HT2A (primary), 5-HT2C, 5-HT1A
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Principal psychoactive alkaloid of peyote (Lophophora williamsii); long duration (10-12 h); ~30x weaker than LSD by weight
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US); legal in ceremonial peyote use (Native American Church). Historical psychiatric research in 1950s-60s; minimal modern trials.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: 5-HT2A, 5-HT2C (Shulgin 'magical half-dozen')
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: Sulfur-substituted phenethylamine psychedelic; longer duration (8–15 h) than 2C-B
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US, 2004). Linked to recreational overdose deaths. No clinical trials.

Primary_Target_Systems_Literature: 5-HT2A (long-acting agonist), 5-HT2C, TAAR1
Mechanism_of_Action_Detail: α-methyl group blocks MAO degradation; duration 14–20 h; very high 5-HT2A affinity
Clinical_Trials_Indications: Schedule I (US). Distributed briefly in 1967 as 'STP' (Serenity-Tranquility-Peace). No clinical trials.
